title: Why crime will decline in (most of) Brazil - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Brazil is rapidly building out facial-recognition surveillance infrastructure -- led by Sao Paulo's police system and hundreds of other projects nationwide -- now covering an estimated 99 million people, more than 47% of the population, with far greater enthusiasm for the technology than seen in other democracies.

insights.claim: Sao Paulo's facial-recognition policing system, introduced in 2024, scans city streets to spot people wanted by police, issues alerts, and dispatches officers, drawing feeds from cameras on street corners, in health centers, on buses, and mounted on police motorbikes into a central control room.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Describes a functioning, integrated urban surveillance system that could serve as a template (or cautionary case) for other cities considering similar crime-tech deployments.

insights.claim: The Sao Paulo facial-recognition system can also locate missing persons, identify stolen vehicles, and provide footage to police investigations, beyond just spotting wanted suspects.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Shows the multi-purpose commercial/public-safety use case for facial-recognition and camera-network vendors beyond pure suspect-tracking.
insights.locator: The system can also locate people

insights.claim: The number of cameras in Sao Paulo's facial-recognition network is set to roughly double to 100,000 by 2028.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A concrete growth trajectory for surveillance-camera infrastructure spend in a major Latin American city, relevant to security-tech vendors and civil-liberties observers.
insights.locator: By 2028 the number of cameras in the network

themes.theme: Nationwide scale of Brazilian facial recognition

insights.claim: A watchdog group, O Panoptico, counts 560 active facial-recognition projects across more than 20 Brazilian states, spanning not just policing but also non-security uses such as school attendance-taking.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Shows facial recognition adoption in Brazil is broad-based across sectors, not confined to law enforcement, widening the addressable market for the technology.
insights.locator: Researchers for O Panoptico, a watchdog

insights.claim: Brazil's facial-recognition systems collectively surveil an estimated 99 million people, more than 47% of the country's population.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of biometric surveillance coverage already achieved in Brazil, a data point for assessing both the market size for the technology and its civil-liberties footprint.

insights.claim: Brazil stands out even among other democracies for its outsized enthusiasm specifically for face-spotting technology, beyond the more common police adoption of body-worn cameras and gunshot-detection microphone networks seen elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Positions Brazil as an outlier case study in democratic-country facial-recognition adoption, useful for comparative policy and market analysis.
insights.locator: What sets Brazil apart from many democracies
